g.data-narration: Welcome to MIMD Systems in Parallel Computing. This diagram explains the most common parallel computing architecture used today.
MIMD is a parallel computing architecture where multiple processors execute different instructions on different data simultaneously.
Unlike SIMD where all processors execute the same instruction, MIMD offers greater flexibility for handling irregular problems.
Shared Memory MIMD systems have all processors sharing a common global address space, communicating through memory reads and writes.
Distributed Memory MIMD systems have each processor with its own local memory, communicating through message passing networks.
Shared memory offers simpler programming but limited scalability, while distributed memory scales well but requires explicit message passing.
Interconnection networks like Bus and Mesh connect processors. Bus is simple but limited; Mesh scales well and is used in supercomputers.
Hypercube offers high connectivity with complex wiring, while Crossbar provides non-blocking connections but at high cost.
Cache coherence ensures multiple cache copies of the same data remain consistent, critical for correct execution in shared memory MIMD.
g.text: Cache Coherence in Shared Memory MIMD
Write-Invalidate: Invalidate other copies when writing
Write-Update: Update other copies with new value

g.data-narration: MSI Protocol has three states: Modified (exclusive write), Shared (read-only), and Invalid (stale data). These states manage cache coherence.
g.text: BusRd → Shared (if other has it) | BusRdX → Invalid

g.data-narration: MESI adds an Exclusive state to MSI, representing a clean single copy. This optimization reduces unnecessary memory transactions.
OpenMP is used for shared memory MIMD programming, using compiler directives to parallelize code with shared variables.
MPI is used for distributed memory MIMD programming, enabling communication between processes through message passing.
Real-world MIMD systems include multicore processors like Intel Core i7 for shared memory, and supercomputers like IBM Summit for distributed memory.
